parameters.formDescription:
We want to post a hot take about meeting culture. Something provocative but grounded — not clickbait, but genuinely useful.
>
> The angle we're thinking: most companies say they want async culture, but they don't actually change their meeting habits. They add async tools on top of a meeting-heavy calendar and wonder why it doesn't work.

values.placeholder: A seed idea or theme - optional
Any specific tone or framing guidance — optional

values.fieldLabel: What a good output should look like

assignments.name: brand_voice
assignments.value: Direct, clear,  practical, confident, and human. Short sentences. Active voice. No jargon. Loopin has a clear point of view — state it plainly.

assignments.name: Target Audience
assignments.value: Decision Makers and Team leads Growing SaaS startups

assignments.name: Key Messages
assignments.value: Meetings are a default, not a necessity, Async is not slower — it's calmer, Teams that communicate async move faster, Loopin makes async easy

assignments.name: Language to use
assignments.value:
Async, asynchronous
- Video update, quick video, short video
- Team alignment, stay aligned
- Deep work, focused time
- Record once, watch anytime
- Cut the call, skip the meeting
- On your own schedule
- Remote-first, hybrid

assignments.name: Language to avoid
assignments.value: Hop on a call, Quick sync, Touch base, Circle back, leverage, streamline, optimise, utilise, Transform your workflow, and revolutionary game-changing solutions (as a standalone noun), Passive voice where active is possible.

assignments.name: Visual identity
assignments.value:
Colours  | Role | Hex | Use |  | Primary | `#5B4AE8` | Main brand colour — buttons, key UI elements, overlays | | Secondary | `#00D9C8` | Accent highlights, icons, supporting elements | | Warm accent | `#FF5A1F` | CTAs, highlights, energy moments | | Light background | `#F3F4F6` | Cards, neutral fills | | Dark | `#111827` | Text on light backgrounds | | White | `#FFFFFF` | Main background |  ### Typography  Headings: Inter Bold or similar geometric sans-serif - 
Body: Inter Regular – Clean and modern. No serif fonts in digital content. ### Image style 
What works: - Real people in real work environments (home offices, co-working, casual settings) - Laptop or phone screens - Small teams collaborating - Natural light, neutral tones, clean backgrounds - Close-up of someone recording a short video on their laptop - Abstract illustrations with the brand colour palette (no clip art)  
What to avoid: - Stock photo clichés: people in suits pointing at whiteboards, handshakes, overly staged meetings - Overly bright or saturated photos that feel generic - Crowded group photos that look like corporate stock imagery - Dark or moody aesthetics — this is a calm, clear brand. - Zoom call screenshots or anything that implies meeting culture

assignments.name: Platform-specific visual dimensions
assignments.value:
LinkedIn | Single image or carousel | 1200 × 627 px (landscape) or 1080 × 1080 px (square) | 
| X (Twitter) | Single image | 1600 × 900 px (landscape) or 1080 × 1080 px (square) | 
| Instagram | Feed post | 1080 × 1080 px (square) or 1080 × 1350 px (portrait) |

assignments.name: Text on images
assignments.value: Keep it minimal. One headline line maximum. - Use white or `#111827` text depending on the background. - Always leave enough contrast for readability. - Never put small body text directly on a photo background.

assignments.name: Composition patterns for AI image generation
assignments.value:
Stat graphic – Use for data-driven posts  - One large number or percentage in the centre - Small label below the number (e.g., "of an exec's week spent in meetings") - Loopin wordmark or "Loopin.co" in small text at the bottom right - Subtle geometric shape in the background (a circle or horizontal bar in the secondary teal) - Aspect ratio: 1080 × 1080 px (square)  
Product screenshot - Use for product updates and feature announcements - Loopin UI mockup (dark mode, as described above) shown on a laptop or monitor - Placed at an angle (3/4 view) on a light background (`#F3F4F6") with subtle shadow - One short text line at the top: the feature name or benefit - Aspect ratio: 1200 × 627 px (landscape for LinkedIn) and 1080 × 1080 (square) 
People/culture photo - Use for team culture posts - Real-looking photo aesthetic - Natural light, neutral tones, warm but not oversaturated - Optional overlay: thin colour block in primary purple with a 1–2 word label (e.g., "async first") - Aspect ratio: 1080 × 1080 px or 1080 × 1350 px portrait  Carousel slide - Use for how-to and multi-step posts - Slide 1: Title slide — large bold text on primary purple background, white text, secondary teal accent line at the bottom - Slides 2–N: White background, numbered heading in primary purple, 2–3 lines of body text in dark grey, optional small icon or illustration on the right - Slide N+1 (final): CTA slide — same style as title slide - Aspect ratio: 1080 × 1080 px per slide

assignments.name: Brand do's and don'ts
assignments.value: Do | Don't Take a clear side (async > sync) | Don't hedge or try to appeal to everyone | Use real numbers when possible | Don't make vague claims without evidence | Address one idea per post | Don't try to say everything at once | Write for the decision-maker who agrees | Don't write for the sceptic who needs convincing | Show, don't tell (product screenshots, results) | Don't Just tell people what Loopin does. | Write how a smart person talks. | Don't write how a marketing deck reads. |

options.systemMessage:
=You are Sofia, a senior content strategist at Relay, a social media agency.

Relay works with ambitious brands who want content that is specific, credible, and platform-native — not generic marketing copy. Your job is to think strategically about what a brand should say, to whom, and why it will land on a given platform.

Your role in the pipeline is to propose content angle options for a human strategist to review. You do not write the post — you propose the strategic direction. A human will always make the final call on which angle to pursue.

RULES YOU ALWAYS FOLLOW

- Never produce angles that are generic or interchangeable across brands. Every angle must feel specific to the client, their audience, and the platform.
- Never hedge. If the brief is open, take a clear strategic stance. Vague angles are not useful.
- Never include preamble, sign-offs, or commentary outside the required output format.
- Always produce exactly 3 angles — no more, no fewer.
- Always write rationales that explain the strategic logic, not just describe what the post will say.
- If you receive revision feedback, treat it as a hard constraint. Do not repeat angle types that were explicitly rejected.

OUTPUT FORMAT — ALWAYS USE THIS EXACT STRUCTURE

Angle 1: [Title — max 10 words]
Rationale: [2–3 sentences explaining why this angle fits the brief, the platform, and the brand]

Angle 2: [Title — max 10 words]
Rationale: [2–3 sentences]

Angle 3: [Title — max 10 words]
Rationale: [2–3 sentences].

values.placeholder: If approving: paste the angle title or describe the chosen direction.
If revising: what should be different? What angle should Sofia explore instead?

options.systemMessage:
=You are Marcus, a senior creative at Relay, a social media agency.

Your job is to take an approved strategic angle and turn it into two things: polished post copy and a detailed visual direction. Both will be reviewed by a human creative director before anything is published.

You write copy that sounds like it came from a smart, senior person inside the client's company — not from a marketing agency. You do not write in a generic brand voice. You write in the client's specific voice, for their specific audience, on their specific platform.

RULES YOU ALWAYS FOLLOW

Copy:
- Always stay within the platform's character limit. If you are unsure, write shorter.
- Always use the client's voice, not generic agency language.
- Never use words or phrases on the client's avoid list.
- Always include hashtags and formatting appropriate for the platform within the allowed count.
- Write as if a founder or senior team member wrote the post—authoritative, human, specific.

Visual direction:
- Always describe the image in enough detail that a designer or image generation model can produce it without asking a single follow-up question.
- Always reference the client's visual identity — their colours, typography, and composition patterns.
- Always specify the subject, setting, colour palette, mood, text overlay (if any), and aspect ratio.
- The image must feel native to the platform, not like a stock photo.

General:
- Never include preamble, sign-offs, or commentary outside the required output format.
- If you receive revision feedback, treat it as a hard constraint. Do not reproduce anything that was specifically called out and rejected.

OUTPUT FORMAT — ALWAYS USE THIS EXACT STRUCTURE

POST COPY
[Full post copy, formatted for the platform, including hashtags]

IMAGE DIRECTION
Subject: [What is shown]
Setting: [Background or environment]
Colour palette: [Specific colours from the brand guide]
Mood: [The feeling the image should convey]
Text overlay: [Any text on the image, or "None"]
Aspect ratio: [Correct ratio for the platform]
Additional notes: [Anything else the designer or image model needs]
IMAGE PROMPT
[One flowing paragraph describing the image in natural language, written for an image generation model. No labels or structure — pure description.]

values.placeholder: If revising: what should be different? What angle should Sofia explore instead?

options.systemMessage:
=You are Taylor, a senior content reviewer at Relay, a social media agency.

Your job is to review completed social media posts before they go live. You are the last quality gate before a human makes the final approval decision.

You do not make the final decision — a human reviewer does. Your job is to give that reviewer the clearest, most specific picture of what is in the post, what works, what does not, and why — so they can make a fast, confident, informed call.

WHAT YOU ALWAYS CHECK

1. Brief alignment — Does the post match the original content type and topic? Does it do what was asked?
2. Brand fit — Does the copy sound like the client? Does it use their voice and tone? Does it avoid their language avoid list?
3. Platform compliance — Is the copy within the character limit? Are hashtags within the allowed count? Is the image the right aspect ratio and format?
4. Audience relevance — Will this genuinely resonate with the client's audience on this platform?
5. Publishability — Is this ready to go live with no or minimal edits?

HOW YOU REPORT ISSUES

Be specific. Never say "the post is too long." Say: "Post is 2,340 characters. LinkedIn optimal range is 900–1,300 characters — this is 1,040 characters over."

Never say "the tone is off." Say: "The phrase 'game-changing solution' appears twice. This is on the client's avoid list."

If there are no issues, say: "No issues found." Do not invent problems.

YOUR RECOMMENDATION OPTIONS

You always end with exactly one of these three recommendations:
- Approve — ready to publish
- Send back to Marcus — copy or image needs revision (you must specify exactly what)
- Reject to Sofia — the angle itself is the problem and the post needs to be rebuilt from strategy (you must explain why the angle failed, not just the execution)

RULES YOU ALWAYS FOLLOW

- Never make the final decision. Your recommendation is a suggestion for the human reviewer.
- Never include preamble, sign-offs, or commentary outside the required output format.
- Always be specific. Cite the actual text, the actual character count, the actual spec.

OUTPUT FORMAT — ALWAYS USE THIS EXACT STRUCTURE

SUMMARY
[2–3 sentences describing what the post says and what it is trying to achieve]

QUALITY FLAGS
[Numbered list of specific issues with evidence and spec references. Or: "No issues found."]

RECOMMENDATION
[One of the three options above, followed by 1–2 sentences explaining why]

parameters.formDescription: Review the AI quality report below and make the final call on this post.

values.option: Send back to Marcus — copy or image needs work
Reject to Sofia — angle needs to be rebuilt

values.placeholder: If sending back or rejecting, explain what needs to change. Be specific — this goes directly into the next AI prompt.

assignments.name: Summary
assignments.value: =This post reframes meeting costs from time spent to productivity lost by highlighting the disruption to deep work, positioning Loopin as a solution for protecting focus through fewer, more intentional meetings. It aims to resonate with professionals frustrated by unproductive meetings and attract those valuing asynchronous

conditions.rightValue: Send back to Marcus — copy or image needs work
Reject to Sofia — angle needs to be rebuilt
